content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
. Sacramento, CA 958 [2-0807
(916) 322-5660
. Fax (916) 322-0886
July 7, 1999
David Bauer
400 Capitol Mall, Suite 1560
Sacramento, CA 95814
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-150
Dear Mr. Bauer:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTION
Should any of the information required on the Ballot Measure Committee Disclosure
Statement (Form 419) filed by Tom McClintock’s Committee to Stop the Car Tax and Keeping
the Promise Initiative Committee, Sponsored by People’s Advocate be aggregated for reporting
purposes?
CONCLUSION
Based on the facts you have provided, it appears that the two committees do not make
joint decisions on expenditures. If this is the case, and the Keeping the Promise Initiative
Committee is not controlled by Assemblymember McClintock, the two committees are not
required to aggregate contributions received from the same source, nor expenditures made for the
same good or service, on their reporting forms.
FACTS
You are the treasurer for Tom McClintock’s Committee to Stop the Car Tax and for
Keeping the Promise Initiative Committee, Sponsored by People’s Advocate. The two
committees are primarily formed to support the qualification of a ballot measure to eliminate
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


David Bauer
Our File No. A-99-150
July 7, 1999
California’s annual automobile registration tax. In addition, the two committees have
coordinated their efforts by jointly developing “tailcing points” and campaign strategy, as well as
other issues related to the initiative.
ANALYSIS
Section 82016 defines a controlled committee as:
“A committee which is controlled directly or indirectly by a
candidate or state measure proponent or which acts jointly with a
candidate, controlled committee or state measure proponent in
connection with the making of expenditures. A candidate or state
measureproponent controls a committee fhe, his agent or any
other committee he controls has a signflcant influence on the
actions or decisions ofthe committee.” [Emphasis added.]
Section 84102(e) requires a sponsored committee to list any controlled committees with
which it acts jointly, so that members ofthe public are put on notice that the resources of the two
committees may be coordinated. We have also previously advised that contributions received
from a single source by committees controlled by the same candidate/officeholder must be
cumulated for reporting purposes. (Weldy Advice Letter, No. 1-92-124.)
From the information provided in your letter, it appears that the two committees are
simply in communication with one another, and neither committee is directing the expenditures
of the other. Therefore, it does not appear that Assemblymember McClintock controls both
committees.
Finally, we have advised that when two committees exist to support or oppose the same
ballot measure, simply coordinating their activity does not mean that all coordinated
expenditures are non-monetary contributions to the other committee. (See Bagatelos Advice
Letter, No. 1-90-666.) For example, if the two committees place an advertisement in a newspaper
and both committees submit checks to the newspaper for their equal portion of the costs, neither
committee would be making a non-monetary contribution to the other.


David Bauer
Our File No. A-99-150
July 7, 1999
If you have additional questions, please feel free to contact me at 916/322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
By:
Moen, PhD
Political Reform Consultant
